items.title: Go Interface Pitfalls: When nil != nil
items.author: Leapcell
items.content:
During the process of coding in Go, we may encounter a situation where, when using an interface type parameter (such as or ) to receive other parameters, the given parameter value is clearly , yet the inequality check does not hold. Why is this the case? This article will reveal the mystery.

isNil:  true
isNil:  false
b == nil:  true

The Go code example above demonstrates some subtle differences when using the type (i.e., ) to determine whether a variable is . Specifically, it shows why, in some cases, a variable of type is not considered even if its value is .

In this code sample, an function is defined with a parameter . Then, in the function, two variables and of different types are initialized, both assigned the value . Next, they are passed as arguments to the function. Additionally, the result of is printed directly for comparison.

From the output, we can see that inside the function, evaluates to , while evaluates to . However, outside the function, evaluates to . This is because within the function, the parameter is of type , which causes to evaluate as . To understand the specific reason, we need to look at the internal structure of . Details below.

The Internal Structure of any (interface{})

In Go, is an alias for . Internally, an value consists of two parts: the and the .

  • : The concrete type held by the interface. If the interface holds a value of type , then the type part is .
  • : The actual value held by the interface. If the value is the integer , then this part is ; if it’s , then it’s .

When a value is assigned to an interface type (like ), the interface stores both the type and the actual value. Only when the type part and the value part are is the interface considered to be .

Going back to the earlier code example, when the value of variable is assigned to an interface variable , the internal structure of becomes and . Therefore, evaluates to .

Checking for nil Using Reflection

Since or cannot always reliably determine whether an interface type is , how can we solve this problem? The answer is: by using .

With reflection, we can directly check whether the value of a variable is .

This article delves into why, when using Go, a variable of interface type may not be considered even when its value is . Through specific code examples and an analysis of the internal structure of (i.e., ), we uncover the essence of this phenomenon.

  • Interface type internal structure: An (i.e., ) is composed of a and a at the underlying level. An interface is considered only when parts are .
  • : Use reflection to accurately determine whether an interface-type variable is .

items.title: How to Reduce Duplication of Method Implementations in Go
items.author: Generatecode
items.content:
In Go, when you have multiple struct types that need to perform the same methods, it can often lead to code duplication. This issue is quite common, especially in cases where you have several struct types with similar methods. In this article, we will explore how to effectively reduce method duplication using interfaces and composition, thereby making your code cleaner and more maintainable.

Understanding the Problem

Let's begin by analyzing the problem you've presented. You have several structs (, , , and ) that all implement the same methods, specifically and . Repetition of code leads to maintenance challenges—any change in method logic must be mirrored across all structs, increasing the risk of errors.

The initial code example you provided clearly outlines the repetitive nature of the method implementations:

func(i *A) Add(x int) {
   i.Qty = append(i.Qty, x)
}
func(i *B) Add(x int) {
   i.Qty = append(i.Qty, x)
}
func(i *C) Add(x int) {
   i.Qty = append(i.Qty, x)
}
func(i *D) Add(x int) {
   i.Qty = append(i.Qty, x)
}

Solution 1: Using Interfaces and Composition

While Go does not support inheritance like some other languages, you can utilize interfaces and struct embedding to achieve a similar effect. Instead of implementing methods for each struct, you can create a base struct to hold the shared logic and use it in your other structs.

Here’s how you can do that:

type Base struct {
   Qty []int
}

You can then embed this base struct into your specific structs:

type A struct {
   Base
}
type B struct {
   Base
}
type C struct {
   Base
}
type D struct {
   Base
}

Implement Methods on Base Struct

Next, you implement the methods on the struct:

func (b *Base) Add(x int) {
   b.Qty = append(b.Qty, x)
}

func (b *Base) Remove(x int) {
   for i, v := range b.Qty {
       if v == x {
           b.Qty = append(b.Qty[:i], b.Qty[i+1:]...)
           break
       }
   }
}

Now, both , , , and can leverage and methods without duplicating the logic:

func main() {
   a := &A{}
   a.Add(5)
   fmt.Println(a.Qty) // Output: [5]
}

Solution 2: Using Interfaces with a Method

Another way to encapsulate the behavior is by utilizing interfaces. By creating an interface, you can define an abstraction that holds the required methods for any struct that conforms to it.

type Adder interface {
   Add(int)
   Remove(int)
}

You can then create structs like , , , and , implementing the and methods as described above, and use a common interface reference to call these methods. However, the method implementation will still remain in each struct, making it less efficient than the first approach but useful in certain scenarios.

Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ)

  • What if I want to add more methods in the future? If you need to add more methods, just implement them in the struct, and all your embedded structs will automatically inherit them.

  • Can I use generics in Go to avoid duplicating methods? Security of types in Go prohibits defining a generic method like some other languages. However, you can use the interface approach as outlined.

By utilizing struct embedding or interfaces in Go, you can significantly reduce code duplication while maintaining a clean and maintainable codebase. The first solution using base structs is often the most effective, especially as the number of struct types increases. This will not only save you time during development but will also reduce potential bugs in your code.

With the techniques mentioned in this article, you can work more efficiently in Go without the headache of repetitive code. Always aim for clearer, more structured code to enhance both functionality and readability.

items.title: Mastering Go Slices: A Deep Dive from Zero to Hero
items.author: Shrijith Venkatramana
items.content:
Go’s slices are a cornerstone of the language, offering a flexible way to work with sequences of data. They’re not arrays, but they’re built on top of them, and understanding how they work under the hood can level up your Go programming. This post breaks down slices from the basics to advanced use cases, with clear examples and practical tips. Let’s dive in.

A slice in Go is a that provides a window into an underlying array. Unlike arrays, which have a fixed length, slices are dynamic, meaning you can grow or shrink them (within limits). A slice is defined by three components: a to the array, a (number of elements accessible), and a (total elements in the underlying array).

Here’s a simple example to show a slice in action:

Slices are declared with , and you can create them using literals (like above) or the function. The Go blog has a great deep dive on slice internals if you want more.

Creating Slices: The Many Ways

You can create slices in several ways, each with its own use case. Here’s a breakdown:

Quick setup with known values
s := make([]int, len, cap)Pre-allocate length and capacity
Work with part of an existing array
Initialize an empty slice (no allocation)

Here’s a complete example showing different creation methods:

: Use when you know the size upfront to avoid reallocations.

Slicing Syntax: Cutting Arrays Like a Pro

The slicing syntax lets you create a new slice from an array or another slice. The index is inclusive, and is exclusive. You can also use a third index, , to control the capacity.

: The capacity of a slice is determined by the underlying array’s length from the index to either the array’s end or the index.

Appending to Slices: Growing with

The function is how you add elements to a slice. If the underlying array has enough capacity, uses it. Otherwise, Go allocates a new, larger array. This can impact performance, so pre-allocating with is often better for large slices.

Notice how the capacity doubles (2 → 4 → 8) when the slice grows. The Go spec explains this behavior.

: Always assign the result of back to the slice, as it may return a new slice.

Copying Slices: Avoiding Shared Data Pitfalls

Slices share their underlying array, which can lead to unexpected behavior. The function creates a new slice with its own array, copying elements from the source.

Here’s an example showing the difference:

: Use when you need an independent slice to avoid modifying the original data.

Slice Gotchas: Common Mistakes to Avoid

Slices are powerful but can trip you up. Here are common pitfalls:

Changes in one slice affect othersUse or create a new slice
Accessing elements in a nil sliceCheck for or initialize

Example of a shared array issue:

: Always be aware of the underlying array when working with multiple slices.

Performance Tips: Making Slices Efficient

Slices are fast, but misuse can slow your program. Here are tips to optimize:

  • : Set length and capacity to avoid reallocations.
  • : Batch appends to reduce array copying.
  • : Only copy what you need to avoid unnecessary overhead.

Example of pre-allocation vs. dynamic growth:

: Pre-allocation reduces memory allocations and improves performance.

Practical Slice Patterns: Real-World Uses

Slices shine in real-world scenarios. Here are two common patterns:

  1. : Create a new slice with elements matching a condition.
  2. : Split a slice into smaller slices.

Where to Go Next with Slices

Slices are a fundamental part of Go, and mastering them opens up efficient, idiomatic coding. To deepen your understanding, try these steps:

  • : Write small programs to test slice behavior, like resizing or sharing arrays.
  • : The Go runtime’s slice implementation (in ) reveals how and growth work.
  • : Use Go’s tool to spot slice-related performance issues.
  • : Look at how packages like or use slices.

Slices may seem simple, but their flexibility and performance make them a powerful tool in every Go developer’s toolkit. Keep practicing, and you’ll be slicing like a pro.

items.title: Designing Go-routines
items.author: Sahil Sarwar
items.content:
Originally published on my Substack blog Brain Bytes and Binary. I deep-dive into Go internals, backend systems, and more.

To understand the architecture that shapes goroutines, we need to understand an overstated but often ignored topic: Concurrency vs. Parallelism.

You must have seen the image below hundreds of times.

Concurrency and parallelism are often confused, but they are fundamentally different concepts. While parallelism refers to executing multiple tasks simultaneously (limited by the number of CPU cores), concurrency is about structuring a program to handle multiple tasks efficiently, even if they are not running simultaneously.

We can only run a certain number of processes simultaneously (equal to the number of CPU cores).

Designing an Efficient Concurrency Model

For an effective concurrency model, we need:

  • The ability to handle a large number of concurrent tasks.
  • A lightweight threading mechanism (more efficient than OS threads).

This leads us to the following hierarchy:

  • A CPU core can have multiple OS threads.
  • A thread can manage numerous goroutines.

However, only a limited number of goroutines can run in parallel (equal to the number of CPU cores).

So, how does the hierarchy look in our concurrency model now?

But I will highlight the fact again that from the above example, it’s evident that we can only run 2 goroutines truly in parallel (since we only have 2 cores).

How do we make sure that 1 thread can handle multiple goroutines concurrently?

We can do that by making some kind of scheduler that can schedule the goroutines, maintains its state, checks if it needs CPU/memory or it needs to terminate it.

The Go Scheduler and G-M-P Model

The goroutine scheduler works in the following G-M-P model -

A Goroutine represents a lightweight user-space thread.

It contains registers, a small stack (starting at 2KB), and a pointer to a P (Processor).

The runtime schedules Gs dynamically on available Ps.

Represents an OS thread that executes Goroutines.

An M can be blocked (when calling a system function, I/O, or network calls), and the runtime may create a new M if needed.

An M can execute only one Goroutine at a time but can switch between them.

Represents a logical CPU and is responsible for scheduling Go routines.

The number of Ps is equal to GOMAXPROCS, which determines the maximum number of Goroutines running in parallel.

Each P has its local run queue (holding Goroutines ready to execute).

This way, we can make sure the scheduler efficiently maps thousands of goroutines onto a limited number of OS threads. This is how Go achieves high performance without the overhead of traditional threading models.

Let’s look at how Go makes sure it utilizes the CPU to its maximum.

Efficient CPU Utilization

Let’s say we have 2 Processors -

P1 - running 30 goroutines

P2 - running 50 goroutines

It’s natural to assume that P1 might complete all its goroutines quicker than P2. What then?

Once a P exhausted its list of goroutines, it can steal other P(s) goroutines and run them instead, making sure we reduce contention and increase CPU utilization.

We can also keep a global queue (where goroutines waiting for execution are stored) from which the P(s) can pick goroutines once their run queue gets exhausted.

We know goroutines are lightweight, but why is that so? And what makes them fast?

Let’s look at memory footprints for goroutines.

Memory Management in Goroutines

Unlike OS threads that start with a fixed-size stack (around 2MB), Goroutines start with a small, dynamically growing stack (around 2KB).

When a Goroutine runs out of stack space, it gradually increases its stack memory.

Instead of allocating a larger contiguous stack (like OS threads), Go performs a process called stack splitting:

  • A new segment of memory is allocated.
  • The previous stack’s contents are copied to the new segment.
  • The old stack can be garbage-collected if unused.

Advantages of Stack Splitting

Memory Efficiency – It avoids allocating large, unused memory upfront.

Less Fragmentation – Since new segments are added dynamically, it avoids fragmentation issues in normal threading models.

Faster Allocation – Since Go doesn’t need to reserve a large stack initially, Goroutines can be created faster.

Now, our goroutines are created. But how can we be sure that it is run at its full potential?

Let’s look at how the scheduler ensures fairness when running the goroutines.

Scheduling: Preemption & Fairness

Preemption is the ability of the scheduler to interrupt a running Goroutine and switch to another one to ensure fairness.

Preemptive scheduling is used to interrupt long-running Goroutines automatically.

The runtime inserts safe points in the function where preemption can occur.

Internally, Go uses a round-robin scheduling approach within each P, along with preemption.

How Preemption Works in Go

When a Goroutine runs for too long without exiting, the scheduler sets a preemption flag on it.

The Goroutine is interrupted at a safe point (normally at the beginning of a function).

The runtime scheduler then moves it to the global run queue, allowing other Goroutines to run.

How Safe Points Are Determined

The compiler inserts preemption checks in function prologues (prologues - prolouge is what happens at the beginning of a function. Its responsibility is to set up the stack frame of the called function).

These checks are low-cost and allow the runtime to preempt a Goroutine when needed.

However, certain functions (long loops without function calls) may not have safe points, making them harder to preempt.

Blocking Tasks in Goroutines

When a Goroutine encounters blocking tasks (e.g., I/O, network calls), the scheduler must track its state efficiently.

How Go Handles Blocking Tasks

The scheduler not only swaps the goroutines from multiple queues, it also maintains the states of the goroutines, just as the CPU maintains the states of processes when swapping different processes.

A Goroutine is paused when it:

  • It is preempted by the scheduler.
  • Blocks on I/O, locks, or network calls.

Once the blocking operations are done, it will mark the goroutines as “READY” and schedule them to the ready queue for the P (processors).

This begged a question in my head.

If a goroutine is making an HTTP request, this is a blocking call, meaning the goroutine gets swapped for another.

Then how does the scheduler know if that HTTP request was successful and the data is available now so that it can restart the goroutine?

I/O Polling and Netpoller

Go uses OS-level pollers (e.g., epoll on Linux, kqueue on macOS) to detect when data is available.

Instead of checking manually, Go relies on event-driven notifications from the OS.

When data is ready, the netpoller wakes up the Goroutine and schedules it for execution.

Go uses I/O multiplexing under the hood, which allows a single M (OS thread) to handle many I/O-bound operations concurrently without blocking. The Go runtime uses system calls like select or poll to monitor multiple sockets or file descriptors simultaneously.

Now that we understand how goroutines work and how they are handled, let’s see what happens if we don’t create good goroutines.

Garbage Collection & Goroutines

The Go runtime has an automatic garbage collector (GC) that reclaims memory allocated to unused objects. Go routines can lead to memory leaks if not managed properly (a channel causing a blocked Goroutine). The runtime tracks Go routines, and orphaned Goroutines are eventually cleaned up if they become unreachable.

Performance Considerations

Avoid Goroutine Leaks – Always close channels or use context.WithCancel() to prevent orphaned Go routines

Optimize Channel Usage – Use buffered channels when possible to reduce blocking

Minimize Lock Contention – Use sync.Mutex carefully to avoid performance bottlenecks

Thanks for reading Brain Bytes & Binary! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

  • Go uses a G-M-P model to efficiently schedule Goroutines.
  • Work stealing & global queue help distribute tasks among processors.
  • Stack splitting ensures memory efficiency and quick Goroutine allocation.
  • Preemptive scheduling prevents long-running Goroutines from starving others.
  • I/O multiplexing enables efficient handling of network calls and system I/O.
  • Proper Goroutine management is necessary to avoid memory leaks.

A lot of us agree on the fact that Golang’s concurrency management via goroutines makes it really simple to write concurrent code, but we don’t realize how complex it has been implemented internally to make it look so simple.

I was fascinated when researching this topic and its intricate details.

That’s all for this week, I enjoyed writing this one. I will come back next week with another interesting one.

items.title: How to Create User IP Middleware in Go with Chi?
items.author: Generatecode
items.content:
Creating middleware in Go, especially using the Chi router, offers an efficient way to process HTTP requests. If you're looking to capture user IP addresses for every request, constructing a custom middleware can simplify your routing logic. In this article, we'll explore how to create a middleware function that retrieves the user's IP address and applies it across all routes effortlessly.

Why Use Middleware for IP Tracking?

Middleware functions are a core aspect of web development in Go, particularly with frameworks like Chi. They allow you to add common functionalities to your route handlers without repeating code. By implementing an IP address middleware, you can log or process the user's IP address for purposes like analytics, security, and more.

Creating Your IP Middleware

To develop a user IP middleware function, we need to ensure that it complies with the Chi middleware signature. This means our function should take in and return . Here's how you can do that:

Step 1: Define Your Middleware Function

First, we'll define the middleware function itself. This function will call our function:

package main

import (
    "net/http"
    "github.com/go-chi/chi/v5"
)

// GetClientIpAddr retrieves the client IP address from the request.
func GetClientIpAddr(req *http.Request) string {
    clientIp := req.Header.Get("X-FORWARDED-FOR")
    if clientIp != "" {
        return clientIp
    }
    return req.RemoteAddr
}

// IPMiddleware is the middleware that gets the user's IP and puts it in the context.
func IPMiddleware(next http.Handler) http.Handler {
    return http.HandlerFunc(func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
        clientIp := GetClientIpAddr(r)
        // Store the IP in context or log it here for your needs.
        // Example: log.Printf("Client IP: %s", clientIp)
        // You can also add it to the context if further handling is required.
        next.ServeHTTP(w, r)
    })
}

Step 2: Integrate the Middleware into Your Chi Router

Now that we have our middleware function ready, we need to integrate it into our Chi router setup. Below is an example of how to do this:

func main() {
    r := chi.NewRouter()

    // Applying the IP middleware globally to all routes
    r.Use(IPMiddleware)

    // Define your routes
    r.Get("/", func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
        w.Write([]byte("Hello, World!"))
    })

    r.Get("/about", func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
        w.Write([]byte("This is the about page."))
    })

    // Start the server
    http.ListenAndServe(":8080", r)
}

Step 3: Testing the Middleware

To test the middleware, you can run your Go application and send requests to your endpoints (e.g., , ). You should see the log messages with the IP addresses printed to your console if you enabled logging.

When you send requests like curl http://localhost:8080/, it will print log messages with the client's IP address inside your middleware, demonstrating that the tracking is functioning as intended.

Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ)

Can I access the IP address in handler functions?

Yes, you can also store the IP address in the request's context to make it accessible in your handler functions. Use for this purpose.

What if the application is behind a proxy?

When your application is behind a proxy, always fetch the header to ensure you get the original client IP address correctly. It's essential for accurate analytics.

Is Chi the only router that supports middleware?

No, there are several routers for Go like Gorilla Mux, Echo, and Gin. Each has its own method for middleware integration, but the concept remains similar.

Creating custom middleware in your Go application using the Chi router is both simple and effective for capturing user IP addresses. By encapsulating this logic in a middleware, you remove redundancy and make your code more maintainable. Now you can track users' IPs effortlessly across all routes in your application. Happy coding!

items.title: Deploying and Exposing Go Apps with Kubernetes Ingress, Part 2
items.author: M. Oly Mahmud
items.content:
In Part 1, we used Kubernetes Ingress to route traffic to two Go-based microservices based on specific paths. In this second part, we’ll explore advanced Ingress features.

In this part, we’ll build and deploy two updated Go-based services (API and Web) in the ~/k8s-learning/ingress/ingress-different-route directory. Unlike Part 1, where services handled specific paths ( and ), here both services respond to the root path (). We’ll use Kubernetes Ingress with a rewrite rule to route to the API service and to the Web service, ensuring clean URL handling.

We will build two services using Go:

  • An API service that responds to HTTP GET requests at the root path () with a JSON response.
  • A Web service that responds to HTTP GET requests at the root path () with an HTML response.

Then, we’ll containerize these services with Docker, set up a Kubernetes cluster using Kind, deploy the services, and configure Ingress with path rewriting to route traffic appropriately.

Here’s the directory structure of the project:

k8s-learning/
└── ingress/
    └── ingress-different-route/
        ├── api-service/
        │   ├── Dockerfile
        │   └── main.go
        ├── web-service/
        │   ├── Dockerfile
        │   └── main.go
        └── k8s/
            ├── api-deployment.yaml
            ├── api-service.yaml
            ├── web-deployment.yaml
            ├── web-service.yaml
            ├── go-app-ingress.yaml
            └── kind-cluster-config-with-ingress.yaml

To Initialize the Go Module for This Service, Run: 

 ~/k8s-learning/ingress/ingress-different-route/api-service
go mod init ingress-api-service-different

This command sets up the go module.

Developing the API with Go

The API service is a Go application that responds to HTTP GET requests at the root path () with a JSON response.

  • The API now uses for routing, explicitly handling instead of .
  • It returns a JSON response ({"message": "API Service"}) instead of HTML.

Dockerizing the API Service

The remains similar to Part 1 but uses the updated image name.

go mod tidy

go build  api-service .

apk  add ca-certificates

Build and push the image:

 ~/k8s-learning/ingress/ingress-different-route/api-service
docker build  olymahmudmugdho/ingress-api-service-different:latest 
docker push olymahmudmugdho/ingress-api-service-different:latest

To Initialize the Go Module for This Service, Run: 

 ~/k8s-learning/ingress/ingress-different-route/web-service
go mod init ingress-web-service-different

Developing the Web Service with Go

The Web service responds to HTTP GET requests at the root path () with an HTML response.

  • The Web service now handles instead of , using .
  • The response remains HTML ().

Dockerizing the Web Service

The is identical to Part 1’s structure but uses the updated image name.

go mod tidy

go build  web-service .

apk  add ca-certificates


 ~/k8s-learning/ingress/ingress-different-route/web-service
docker build  olymahmudmugdho/ingress-web-service-different:latest 
docker push olymahmudmugdho/ingress-web-service-different:latest

Setting Up Kubernetes with KIND

The Kind cluster configuration remains identical to Part 1.

kind create cluster  k8s/kind-cluster-config-with-ingress.yaml

Installing the Ingress Controller

Deploy the NGINX Ingress controller, as in Part 1:

kubectl apply  https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes/ingress-nginx/controller-v1.10.1/deploy/static/provider/kind/deploy.yaml

kubectl get pods  ingress-nginx

API Deployment Configuration

The API Deployment is similar to Part 1 but uses the new image.

: Creates a Deployment for the API service, ensuring one replica runs with the new Docker image, exposing port 8080.

kubectl apply  k8s/api-deployment.yaml

The API Service is unchanged from Part 1.

: Exposes the API pods internally via a ClusterIP Service, mapping external port 80 to the container’s port 8080.

kubectl apply  k8s/api-service.yaml

Web Deployment Configuration

The Web Deployment is similar to Part 1 but uses the new image.

: Creates a Deployment for the Web service, ensuring one replica runs with the new Docker image.

The Web Service is unchanged from Part 1.

: Exposes the Web service internally.

Creating Ingress Resource (go-app-ingress.yaml)

The Ingress resource introduces a rewrite rule to handle the new routing logic.

  • : The identifies the Ingress resource. Consider renaming to for consistency with Part 1.
  • : The nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target: / rewrites incoming paths to before forwarding to the backend services. This is critical since both services expect requests at , but external requests use or .
  • : Defines routing rules for .
  • : Set to , suitable for local testing with Kind. In production, this would be a domain (e.g., ).
  • :
    • : Matches URLs starting with . The rewrite rule strips , forwarding the request as to on port 80.
    • : Matches all other URLs (root path). The request is forwarded as to on port 80.
  • : Specifies the target Service ( or ) and port ().
  • : Ensures paths like match , but the rewrite rule normalizes them to .
  • The Web service is now routed at instead of .
  • The annotation handles path rewriting, allowing both services to operate at internally.
  • This setup simplifies external URLs (e.g., instead of ).
kubectl apply  k8s/go-app-ingress.yaml

Verifying Ingress Functionality

Ensure all components are running:

kubectl get deployments
kubectl get services
kubectl get ingress
kubectl describe ingress example-ingress
kubectl get pods  ingress-nginx

curl http://localhost/api


curl http://localhost/


curl http://localhost/invalid

If you face any problem, troubleshoot:

  • Check Ingress controller logs: kubectl logs -n ingress-nginx <pod-name>.
  • Ensure resolves correctly in or network settings.
  • Verify the rewrite rule is applied (kubectl describe ingress example-ingress).

items.title: How to Use Shared Memory in Golang with C Interface?
items.author: Generatecode
items.content:
When developing applications in Golang, especially for high-performance scenarios requiring Inter-Process Communication (IPC), the challenge of efficiently passing large data can arise. In your case, the need to interact with an existing application (App A) using shared memory to process vast amounts of data leads us to explore how we can safely and efficiently implement this in Golang. This article will explain the intricacies of utilizing shared memory with Golang, addressing the potential pitfalls of using C interfaces, and how to resolve them.

Understanding Shared Memory IPC

Shared memory is an IPC method where multiple applications can access the same segment of memory. Unlike other IPC methods, such as pipes or message queues, shared memory allows for faster communication since processes can directly read and write from the same memory space. However, it introduces challenges in memory management and synchronization, which must be handled carefully.

Why Use Go for Application B?

Golang, known for its simplicity and speed, is an excellent choice for building modern applications needing high efficiency and scriptability. Despite Golang's advantages, it lacks direct support for shared memory, necessitating the use of C for this purpose.

The Challenges of Using C Interfaces in Golang

Implementing shared memory through C in Golang can lead to complexities, especially in pointer management. As you've noted, passing pointers between C and Go can cause issues, particularly when dealing with memory allocation and deallocation. Mismanagement can easily lead to runtime crashes, as you've encountered with the line defer C.free(unsafe.Pointer(s)).

Implementing Shared Memory in Golang with C

Let's clarify how to accomplish your goals effectively. I'll provide you with a step-by-step approach to read from shared memory using Go and C. Here's how you can set it up:

Step 1: Create the C Wrapper

Start by creating a simple C file (), which will handle the reading from shared memory. Make sure you handle memory correctly to avoid crashes:

#include <sys/types.h>
#include <stdio.h>
#include <errno.h>

char buf[0x10000];

char* testc(char* filename){
    printf("reading file %s\n", filename);
    FILE *fp = fopen(filename, "r");
    if (fp == NULL) {
        perror("Could not open file");
        return NULL;
    }
    fread(buf, 1, sizeof(buf), fp);
    fclose(fp);
    return buf;
}

In this piece of code, we safely read from the specified file into the . Ensure proper error handling to return on failure.

Step 2: Define the Go Reader

Next, create a Golang file () to interface with your C function:

package main

/* #include <wrapper.h> */
import "C"
import "unsafe"
import "fmt"

func read(filename string) string {
    f := C.CString(filename)
    defer C.free(unsafe.Pointer(f))
    s := C.testc(f)
    if s == nil {
        return "Error reading file"
    }
    return C.GoString(s)
}

func main() {
    fmt.Println(read("tmp"))
}

Here, the function converts a Go string into a C string, calls the C function, and converts the result back to a Go string. Notice that we check if is , which helps prevent crashes due to calling on an invalid pointer.

Common Issues and Solutions

  1. As you found, calling C.free(unsafe.Pointer(s)) can cause crashes due to improper handling of memory allocated by C. Ensure you're not freeing memory allocated for static or global variables in C.
  2. If multiple processes are reading/writing to shared memory, you'll need to implement some form of locking (e.g., mutexes) to prevent race conditions.
  3. File Descriptor Management: Always check if file-related operations succeed, and handle errors appropriately to avoid memory leaks and crashes.

Frequently Asked Questions

Q1: Why can’t I free a non-allocated pointer? A1: Attempting to free a pointer not allocated by can lead to undefined behavior. Always ensure that memory allocation is managed on the C side appropriately.

Q2: How can I share memory across processes? A2: Use shared memory functions from the POSIX API or use memory-mapped files for a more efficient approach than just communicating via standard file I/O.

Using Golang with C for shared memory interaction provides efficiency but comes with increased complexity in memory management. By following proper guidelines, employing good coding practices, and ensuring thorough error handling, you can successfully implement this communication method. Test your application both thoroughly and incrementally, and you'll find that shared memory can provide the performance boost you need for your application.

items.title: How to Resolve Discord Bot HTTP 403 Forbidden DM Issues?
items.author: Generatecode
items.content:
If you're developing a Discord bot using Go and experiencing the error when trying to send direct messages (DMs) to users, you're in the right place. This article will explore common reasons why your bot may not be able to send DMs and provide actionable steps to resolve the issue.

The error message you encountered: HTTP 403 Forbidden, {"message": "Cannot send messages to this user", "code": 50007} indicates that your bot is attempting to send a message to a user who has disabled DMs from server members. This is a common issue and can occur even if the bot is in the same server as the user.

Why Can't My Bot Send DMs?

In Discord, users have the ability to adjust their privacy settings. Here are a few reasons why your bot might be unable to send direct messages:

  1. : Users can block DMs from server members. If this option is disabled, your bot will not be able to send DMs.
  2. : Make sure your bot has the appropriate permissions to send messages in the server and to users.
  3. Intentions and Authorization: Ensure your bot has the right intents enabled to read messages and interact with users effectively.

Steps to Troubleshoot and Fix

To resolve the issue, follow these steps:

Step 1: Check User Settings

  1. : Have the user check their Discord settings by going to User Settings > Privacy & Safety.
  2. : Ensure the option 'Allow direct messages from server members' is enabled. If it is disabled, your bot won’t be able to send DMs.

Step 2: Verify Bot Permissions

  1. : Ensure your bot has permissions to send messages in the server.
  2. : If the bot's role is lower than the user’s role, it might affect sending DMs. Make sure the bot has a higher role.

Step 3: Update Code to Handle Errors Gracefully

While trying to send DMs, it’s good practice to handle situations where sending fails. You can do this by checking if the user has DMs enabled before attempting to send a message. Here’s how you can modify your code:

// commandHandler handles the slash command
func commandHandler(s *discordgo.Session, i *discordgo.InteractionCreate) {
    // Check that it's an application command
    if i.Type != discordgo.InteractionApplicationCommand {
        return
    }

    // Respond to the command with an ephemeral message
    err := s.InteractionRespond(i.Interaction, &discordgo.InteractionResponse{
        Type: discordgo.InteractionResponseChannelMessageWithSource,
        Data: &discordgo.InteractionResponseData{
            Content: "Hello! I can send you a private message.",
            Flags:   1 << 6, // Ephemeral message (only visible to the user)
        },
    })
    if err != nil {
        log.Println("Error sending command response:", err)
        return
    }

    // Check if user can receive DMs
    if !canReceiveDMs(i.Member.User.ID) {
        log.Println("User has DMs disabled.")
        return
    }

    // Attempt to create a DM channel and send a message
    channel, err := s.UserChannelCreate(i.Member.User.ID)
    if err != nil {
        log.Println("Error creating DM channel:", err)
        return
    }

    // Send a direct message to the user
    _, err = s.ChannelMessageSend(channel.ID, "Here is your private message from the bot!")
    if err != nil {
        log.Println("Error sending DM:", err)
    }
}

// canReceiveDMs checks if the bot can send DMs to the user
func canReceiveDMs(userID string) bool {
    // Logic to determine if the user can receive DMs from the bot
    // For simplicity, returning true for the demo
    return true // This should be implemented based on real checks.
}

Step 4: Check Developer Portal Settings

Ensure your bot's settings on the Discord Developer Portal have the necessary intent configured. Navigate to Bot > Privileged Gateway Intents, and enable .

Step 5: Test with Different Users

Sometimes the issue may be limited to specific users. Try sending DMs to other users who are also part of the server to confirm whether the behavior is consistent across multiple user accounts.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I send DMs to users in a group DM?

Yes, as long as the user's privacy settings allow it and you're in the same server.

What if the bot is still unable to send DMs after the settings are correct?

Double-check the bot's permissions, and confirm that the bot's role is not lower than the user roles in your server.

Can I modify the user's DM settings programmatically?

No, DM privacy settings can only be modified by the user through their Discord account settings.

In summary, if your Discord bot is encountering an error while trying to send direct messages, it could be due to user privacy settings, permissions, or even bot permissions. By following the troubleshooting steps outlined in this article, you should be able to successfully configure your bot to send DMs without facing this error. Remember to check user settings, bot permissions, and adequately handle errors in your code to improve user experience.

The tag is used to control which fields should be omitted when serializing Go objects into JSON. Unlike the tag, which omits fields, zero values and empty values, while similar, are not equivalent in Go. For example:

The method is used to customize the logic for determining whether a field is a zero value. If you want to change how a field’s zero value is determined, you can implement the method for that field. Here's an example:

In this example, the field defines its own zero-value logic by implementing the method. If is less than or equal to 0, returns , and the field will be omitted during serialization. The output will be:

In Go, by using the tag, we can precisely control which fields are omitted during JSON serialization, ensuring that only zero-value fields are excluded. items.title: How to Use Reflection with Nested Structs in Go?
items.author: Generatecode
items.content:
Understanding Reflection in Go with Nested Structs

Reflection is a powerful feature in Go that allows you to inspect and manipulate objects at runtime. In this blog post, we'll explore how to use reflection with nested structs and interfaces, specifically focusing on printing the contents of structs that implement a certain interface. We will fix the example provided, ensuring that our code dynamically recognizes interface implementations without explicitly mentioning the structs.

The Problem with Your Reflection Code

In your provided code, you are encountering an 'ErrUnhandledType' error when trying to directly recognize the interface during iteration. This occurs because the method retrieves the value of the struct, not the pointer that implements the interface. Thus, you're unable to correctly switch on the interface type.

To illustrate this better, let’s break down how to structure the code using reflection properly:

1. Define Your Structs and Interface

Here’s a revised version of your , , and interface definitions:

package main

import (
    "log"
    "reflect"
)

// Macro is the main struct containing nested structs
type Macro struct {
    Field1 *Test
    Field2 *Test
}

// PrintStr defines a method for printing struct content
type PrintStr interface {
    Print() // Method that will print the struct content
}

// Test is a simple struct implementing the PrintStr interface
type Test struct {
    str string
}

// Print method implementation
func (t *Test) Print() {
    log.Println(t.str)
}

Notice we’re using pointers for the structs within . This helps achieve behavior where the actual method implementations can be invoked correctly.

2. Implementing the Main Logic with Reflection

Here’s the function that utilizes reflection to call the method on each struct that implements the interface:

func main() {
    test := Macro{
        Field1: &Test{str: "field1"},
        Field2: &Test{str: "field2"},
    }
    
    // Use reflection to iterate through fields
    values := reflect.ValueOf(test)

    // Check if the kind of value is struct
    if values.Kind() == reflect.Struct {
        for i := 0; i < values.NumField(); i++ {
            field := values.Field(i)
            if field.Kind() == reflect.Ptr && !field.IsNil() {
                // Switch-type assertion to interface
                if printer, ok := field.Interface().(PrintStr); ok {
                    printer.Print() // Call the Print method
                } else {
                    log.Fatal("ErrUnhandledType")
                }
            }
        }
    }
}

Explanation of the Code Logic

  1. : We declare of type containing pointers to instances. This is crucial for calling methods defined on the struct.
  2. : We retrieve the type of and check if it’s a struct. If so, we iterate through its fields.
  3. Nil and Type Assertion Check: For each field, we verify if it is a pointer and not . We use a type assertion to check if the field implements the interface and call the method if it does.

Using reflection in Go allows us to work seamlessly with interfaces and dynamically handle types without knowing them at compile time. With this approach, your code becomes more flexible and maintains clean interface segregation, making it easier to extend and manage nested structs. Always remember to handle pointers and nil checks appropriately to avoid runtime panics.

Frequently Asked Questions

Q: What is reflection in Go? A: Reflection in Go is a feature that enables inspection and modification of variables at runtime, providing the ability to work with values of arbitrary types.

Q: Why do I need to use pointers for interface implementations? A: Using pointers allows you to invoke methods defined on structs, as methods declared on struct types need a receiver of the same type.

Q: Can reflection impact performance? A: Yes, using reflection can be slower than direct method calls and should be used judiciously in performance-critical sections of code.

Ensure you test the code in your environment, and don’t hesitate to ask more questions about Go or reflection!

items.title: How can I optimize a Go solution for maximum score problem?
items.author: Generatecode
items.content:
In this article, we will dive into an efficient Go solution for the problem of finding the maximum score from two sorted arrays. This problem can often be found on competitive programming platforms, including LeetCode. We'll discuss the approach of using two pointers to solve the problem efficiently. Additionally, we will explore some optimizations that you can implement to ensure your code handles large inputs without facing overflow issues.

Why Do Overflow Issues Occur?

When working with large datasets, especially in programming languages like Go, it’s crucial to choose the right variable types. In the context of this problem, overflowing can happen if the computed sums exceed the limits of the data type used. Here, we are already using , which should be sufficient for storing large values, but you might still encounter issues depending on how variables are handled during summation.

The core idea of the solution involves traversing two arrays simultaneously. Here's a step-by-step breakdown of how to implement this in Go:

Step 1: Function to Get Maximum

Let's start with a helper function that returns the maximum of two values.

func max(a, b int64) int64 {
    if a > b {
        return a
    }
    return b
}

Step 2: Initialize Variables

Next, in the main function , initialize all necessary variables. This includes counters for both arrays and a mod constant to handle large sums.

func maxSum(nums1 []int, nums2 []int) int {
    var cnt1 int64 = 0
    var cnt2 int64 = 0
    var ans int64 = 0
    p1 := 0
    p2 := 0
    n1 := len(nums1)
    n2 := len(nums2)
    const N int64 = 1e9 + 7
}

To avoid boundary overflow, preprocess both arrays by appending a large value at the end:

var _nums1 [100010]int64
var _nums2 [100010]int64
for i:=0; i<n1; i++ {
    _nums1[i] = int64(nums1[i])
}
for i:=0; i<n2; i++ {
    _nums2[i] = int64(nums2[i])
}
_nums1[n1] = 1e9
_nums2[n2] = 1e9

This part of code will implement a loop to traverse both arrays.

for p1 < n1 || p2 < n2 {
    for _nums1[p1] < _nums2[p2] {
        cnt1 = (cnt1 % N + _nums1[p1] % N) % N
        p1++
    }
    for _nums2[p2] < _nums1[p1] {
        cnt2 = (cnt2 % N + _nums2[p2] % N) % N
        p2++
    }
    if _nums2[p2] == _nums1[p1] {
        ans = (ans % N + max(cnt1, cnt2) % N) % N
        cnt1 = 0
        cnt2 = 0
        if p2 < n2 {
            ans = (ans % N + _nums2[p2] % N) % N
        }
        if p1 < n1 {
            p1++
        }
        if p2 < n2 {
            p2++
        }
    }
}

Finally, return the computed maximum score as follows:

By implementing this two-pointer solution, we can efficiently solve the maximum score problem with large input arrays. The use of for our sum calculations ensures that we can handle vast sums without encountering integer overflow. If you face any unexpected results during testing, double-check your implementation against edge cases, such as arrays with no common elements or varying lengths.

Frequently Asked Questions

What happens if two large arrays cause overflow?

Using should normally be sufficient unless you are summing a large number of elements. Ensure calculations are properly modulo reduced to avoid reaching the maximum limit.

What's the time complexity of this algorithm?

The time complexity is O(n + m) where n and m are the lengths of the two arrays, as we traverse each array only once.

Can this approach handle different data types?

This solution is tailored for arrays, but you can modify it to handle other numeric types if needed, keeping in mind their respective limits.

This implementation passed 80 out of 82 test cases on LeetCode, indicating that while mostly efficient, there may be edge cases to consider when working with large inputs. Test carefully!

And how transactions are added:

Why Go for Blockchain? Concurrency: Goroutines make parallel mining or network synchronization easier to model.

items.title: But why care about SOLID?
items.author: Alessandro Aiezza
items.content:
Or: Why care about Software Engineering principles?

Embracing principles makes you a more adaptable engineer. You’re no longer tied to a specific framework, language, or tech stack. Instead, you can work effectively with the tools at hand and make them .

For example, I appreciate the SOLID principles. When I have the freedom to choose, and it fits the project, I often reach for Kotlin. It makes straightforward in most cases.

SOLID Principles in Kotlin

But what if I need to use something else, like Go? The specific language isn’t the most important thing. As long as the problem is with the available tools, what matters is that they support the principles that guide your work.

I care about the mission and delivering value, not unnecessary complexity or academic perfection. My goal is to leave codebases better than I found them—clear, maintainable, and kind to those who come after me. That’s our responsibility as software engineers.

Software engineering principles and design patterns popularized by leaders in our industry exist for a reason. They’ve done the hard work and generously shared their insights. Why not stand on the shoulders of giants?

Here are those same SOLID principles, but in Go—this time, using functional types where it makes sense:

At first glance, the code looks different. Naturally—it’s a different language with its own syntax and idioms. But the underlying principles remain the same, unlocking the same benefits in your codebase. That’s why it’s always worthwhile to learn the features of the language you’re working in.

    I encourage you: Find ideals and principles that you and your team can support. Then, be open to what that might mean for your code. It may involve using language features that are unfamiliar at first.

    But if it leaves your codebase easier to maintain, more readable, better tested, more modular, or more aligned with the ubiquitous language of your domain—embrace it! You’ll be improving yourself, your team, and your product.

    And if you care about the people for whom you're building, should be motivation enough.

items.title: PocketBase + Firebase Studio
items.author: aaronblondeau
items.content:
I needed an excuse to try out Firebase Studio so I decided to try and develop a PocketBase backend on it. It didn't work right out of the box, but it was fairly easy to get going.

First, create a Go project in Firebase Studio. Use "API server" as the environment.

At the time of writing this, the default version of Go in the environment is 1.21, but PocketBase requires 1.23.

To get a newer version of Go, open up .idx/dev.nix and update the channel to "stable-24.11":

channel = "stable-24.11";

I love that they are using NixOS for these environments as it will make them very customizable.

You should be prompted to rebuild the environment once you save this change.

Once the environment restarts, make sure you have Go 1.23+.

Your environment will have been setup to run your app via Air. You will need to customize Air in order to run PocketBase.

Create an air.toml file by running this command in a terminal window:

Open the air.toml file and set args_bin to "serve"

Navigate to the terminal window that is running air. It will be titled "[onStart] run-server" Use ctrl+c to stop it.

Then in a terminal window run:

In the terminal window that was running air, hit the up key followed by enter to re-run the air command.

You'll notice that the output provides the PocketBase superuser setup url. This url, however, uses http://127.0.0.1:8090 as the host name which you won't have access to.

You'll need to replace the http://127.0.0.1:8090 with your project's public URL. To get this url, first click on the Firebase Studio icon in the left navigation bar. Then expand the "Backend Ports" section.

Click on the lock next to port 8090 to make it publicly accessible.

Then open or copy the link from one of the buttons under "actions".

After constructing the admin setup url it will look something like this:

https://8090-firebase-pocketbasedemo-1746757209594.cluster-rhptpnrfenhe4qarq36djxjqmg.cloudworkstations.dev/_/#/pbinstal/eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJjb2xsZWN0aW9uSWQiOiJwYmNfMzE0MjYzNTgyMyIsImV4cCI6MTc0Njc1OTc0MywiaWQiOiI0MHpjOTU4MHgwbTZjcDIiLCJyZWZyZXNoYWJsZSI6ZmFsc2UsInR5cGUiOiJhdXRoIn0.f7KtQqQ9nVza39RyKi_-QjARkAIq00eooNH3sEW4iak

Open the superuser setup url, complete the setup and you'll be up and running with PocketBase!

Note 1 : Your PocketBase admin URL will be whatever you get from the backend ports section followed by a "/_/". For my environment this was:

https://8090-firebase-pocketbasedemo-1746757209594.cluster-rhptpnrfenhe4qarq36djxjqmg.cloudworkstations.dev/_/

Note 2 : To run PocketBase manually (with air stopped), you'll use the following command. This helps ensure you're using the same pb_data folder that the server uses when running with air:

go run . serve --dir ./tmp/pb_data
